channel.title: FT – Fiction
channel.description: Fiction reviews and articles from the Financial Times.

item.title: The best books of the week
item.description: From British declinism to Antony Beevor on Rasputin and a Chinese factory memoir. Plus fiction by Elizabeth Strout — and Boyd Tonkin on Marilyn Monroe and the power of reading

item.title: Hey Man by Andrew Meehan — from bromance to the brotherhood
item.description: A lonely teenager lodges with his father’s cousin in this story of substitute love and male friendship

item.title: From Lady Chatterley to Marilyn to Dua Lipa, books are in rude health
item.description: Reading for both pleasure and betterment endures — even if unquestioning faith in literary canons and critical authority is gone

item.title: The Things We Never Say by Elizabeth Strout — profound and resplendent
item.description: A new tale about the power of secrets by the Pulitzer Prize-winning author of ‘Olive Kitteridge’

item.title: Other People’s Children by Ben Faccini — an accomplished tale of families, biological or blended
item.description: In a beautifully balanced novel, a man tries to bond with his partner’s young sons while grappling with his own inherited history

item.title: Hey, Good Morning, How Are You? — an unsettling story of art and ageing
item.description: A romance scam takes an unexpected turn in Martina Hefter’s German Book Prize-winning novel, her first translated into English

item.title: Devotions by Lucy Caldwell — stories that wrestle with the biggest question of all
item.description: A hunt for meaning unites the eight tales from one of the best practitioners of the short form

item.title: The full and interesting lives of writers’ alter egos
item.description: Doctors, dairy farmers, failed novelists — how authors’ fictional selves take on an identity all their own, distinct from their creators

item.title: Ghost-Eye — Amitav Ghosh’s ambitious novel about climate change and reincarnation
item.description: The story of a young girl in 1960s Calcutta who claims to have lived before shows the difficulties of grappling with climate politics in literary fiction

item.title: Sex, lies and the trials of being Gen Z — the best new debut novels
item.description: Witty, observant and wildly original stories explore the difficulties of modern dating, dark goings-on in 1950s Chelsea — and a dog’s-eye view of the world

item.title: Alphaville.And the FTAV charts quiz winner is…
item.description: Plotto

item.title: The Shadow of the Object — Chloe Aridjis’s story of metamorphosis, mortality and magic lanterns
item.description: The novelist explores the realm between reality and imagination in a dreamlike tale journeying from Mexico to England

item.title: My Year in Paris with Gertrude Stein — Deborah Levy’s adventures with the avant-garde
item.description: A nameless narrator finds connections with the American pioneer of modernism in a confounding, compelling fiction-biography hybrid

item.title: Interview.Leïla Slimani: ‘This novel was very, very, very difficult to write’
item.description: To finish her semi-autobiographical ‘The Country of Others’ trilogy the French-Moroccan writer drew on deeply personal experiences of exile, prejudice and her father’s wrongful imprisonment

item.title: You Are the Führer’s Unrequited Love — the truth behind the ‘good Nazi’
item.description: In a hybrid of fiction and essay, Jean-Noël Orengo explores how Albert Speer charmed Hitler and then laundered his own postwar reputation

item.title: Stories of superheroes, spellsmiths and southern gothic terror
item.description: Four of the best new sci-fi novels chart a darker journey across America, including a Marvel reboot, a crime caper that fuses magic and linguistics, plus a philosophical view of a looming apocalypse

item.title: FT Magazine.The story behind an almost forgotten 1950s feminist fantasy classic
item.description: Why Naomi Mitchison’s Travel Light gets better with distance.

item.title: She Who Remains — rites and wrongs in the Accursed Mountains
item.description: Rene Karabash’s tale of a ‘sworn virgin’ in a remote corner of Albania is a worthy shortlistee for the International Booker Prize

item.title: The Palm House — Gwendoline Riley’s novel of toxic men and flawed mothers
item.description: The prizewinning author deploys language to devastating effect as she revisits her theme of women plagued by brittle relationships

item.title: What Am I, A Deer? — Polly Barton’s love letter to karaoke
item.description: The translator’s debut novel is a funny, moving and skilfully crafted story of obsession, performance and the fantasies that drive people to action

item.title: What’s the point of an AI novel?
item.description: The danger is not that it will replace human-authored books — but that we stop caring about good writing at all

item.title: Interview.Why Constance Debré shed marriage, law and a French political dynasty to write novels
item.description: One of France’s most significant literary voices on pursuing a ‘higher purpose’ — and how the country’s judicial system crushes the weakest

item.title: Transcription by Ben Lerner — the contested ground between fact and fiction
item.description: The US novelist probes the porous border between the real and the unreal in this thrilling hall of mirrors

item.title: Lázár — a bravura saga of family ghosts, secrets and lies
item.description: Nelio Biedermann’s ambitious debut is a fast-paced story of decline and fall across three generations in Austria-Hungary

item.title: The Witch by Marie NDiaye — spellbinding
item.description: Shortlisted for the International Booker Prize, this dreamlike, unsettling tale explores the terror and sorrow of a mediocre sorceress with meagre talents
